context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Participatory Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What are the ways in which participation can be achieved? What's the measure of participation and how must one judge it? Can the tension between participation as a goal and as a method be resolved and if so how? What were the structural factors that made participation such a key element in development strategies?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m kinda lost on this participatory development reflection tbh. idk how to even start or what the prompt is really asking.
exchanges.tutor: Hey! Let's break this down together. The prompt actually contains several distinct questions. Can you count how many separate questions are being asked in it, and tell me what they are in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling lost. Rather than explaining the prompt to them, I should use the Socratic method to help them unpack it themselves. I'll start by asking them to break down the prompt into its component questions, which will help them self-direct and build confidence. I'll keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: umm maybe like 3? i can’t tell if “measure” and “judge it” are the same thing tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to question that! 'Measure' and 'judge' are closely related, so you can treat them as one idea. So what are the other distinct questions you see in the prompt? Try listing them all out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is engaging with the prompt and showing some analytical thinking. Their uncertainty about 'measure' vs 'judge' is a reasonable observation but could become a distraction from the bigger picture. I should validate their effort, briefly address that nuance, and push them to identify all the questions so we can move forward.

exchanges.student: i think it’s like: how participation happens, how u know if it’s real/good, and then the goal vs method thing? the structural factors part is where i get lost fr.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid breakdown! On structural factors — think about the course themes. Why do you think participation became such a big deal in development specifically? What was happening in the world, or in the development field, that made it feel necessary?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done a decent job identifying the main questions. Now I should affirm their breakdown and push them to think about the 'structural factors' part themselves, rather than explaining it to them. A guiding question can help them connect 'structural factors' to context they likely already have from the course.

exchanges.student: idk, maybe top-down planning wasn’t working? but then i’m not sure if that’s too vague or if i’m missing like colonialism/global stuff rn.
exchanges.tutor: You're pointing in a good direction! Now dig a little deeper — what specifically failed about top-down planning? Who was being left out, and what were the consequences of that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with 'top-down planning wasn't working' and is self-aware enough to wonder if they're missing broader context like colonialism. This is good critical thinking. I should affirm the direction without confirming the full answer, and push them to think more specifically about what the failures of top-down planning looked like and how that connects to the structural factors question.

exchanges.student: yeah see this is where i blank lol, can u just explain the whole participation thing more broadly first?
exchanges.tutor: That's not quite my role here — I'm here to help you think it through, not explain it for you! But you're not as blank as you think. You already said top-down planning left people out. Who are those people, and what happens when they have no say in decisions that affect them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain participation broadly, which would essentially be doing the intellectual work for them. I should redirect them back to their own thinking and the course materials. I should be clear about my role without being harsh, and use a question to get them back on track.

exchanges.student: maybe local communities? and then plans don’t fit real needs i guess, but i still don’t get how to turn that into the paper.
exchanges.tutor: Good thinking! You've already got a real idea there. Now look back at the questions you listed earlier — how does what you just said connect to the 'structural factors' question specifically? Try making that link explicit.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good conceptual progress - they've identified local communities and the mismatch between plans and real needs. Now they're asking how to turn this into a paper, which is a legitimate concern about structure. I should acknowledge their progress and push them to connect their ideas to the actual prompt questions they identified earlier, rather than giving them a structure.

exchanges.student: i’m still mixing everything together tbh. can u at least tell me like the main buckets i should talk about, bc rn it all feels like one giant blob.
exchanges.tutor: You already found the buckets yourself earlier! You listed: how participation happens, how you know if it's real/good, the goal vs. method tension, and structural factors. Does that feel like a possible structure to you? What would go first and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to organize the paper for them, which would be doing their work. However, they've already identified the main questions in the prompt themselves earlier in the conversation. I should redirect them to their own earlier breakdown rather than providing new structure, and encourage them to use that as their organizing framework.

exchanges.student: maybe, but idk what “participation as a goal vs a method” even means tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Let's think about it concretely. Imagine a development project where the planner says 'we need to involve the community in building this.' Is that participation being used as a tool to get a better outcome, or is the involvement itself the whole point? What's the difference between those two things?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on a specific conceptual distinction. This is a legitimate intellectual hurdle worth addressing with a guiding question rather than an explanation. I'll use a concrete hypothetical to help them discover the distinction themselves.

exchanges.student: i guess tool = better project, goal = people having voice? but then aren’t those basically the same lol.
exchanges.tutor: Not quite the same! Think about this: what if involving the community slows down the project, or leads to a decision the planner thinks is 'wrong'? Does the planner override the community? Your answer to that reveals whether participation is truly a goal or just a method.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting to grasp the distinction but collapsing it too quickly. This is a key conceptual tension in the prompt and worth pushing on a little more. I'll ask them to think about what happens when the two conflict, which will help them see they are NOT the same.

exchanges.student: ok wait that helps, but now i’m lost again on how to judge “real” participation vs fake participation tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Good question, but notice we've now unpacked most of the prompt together! Before going deeper on this, I think it's time to start putting pen to paper. What would a first sentence or two of your reflection actually look like, based on everything we've discussed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress conceptually but keeps moving between questions rather than consolidating. We've now covered most of the prompt's questions across the conversation. This is the 3rd distinct conceptual detour and the student hasn't written anything yet. I should briefly engage with this question but then push the student to start integrating their thinking into actual written content, which is what the assignment requires.

deductions.reason: The tutor consistently affirms student progress ('Good instinct,' 'Good thinking,' 'solid breakdown') without providing specific process-level feedback on how the student could strengthen their reasoning approach, structure their evidence use, or improve their analytical method. Praise is given but no concrete methodological guidance is offered.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrates strong Socratic practice throughout, consistently refusing to give direct answers, redirecting the student back to their own thinking, and building on student-generated ideas. The conversation progresses well without redundancy or off-topic drift. The one clear weakness is in meta-learning feedback: the tutor offers only generic affirmations ('Good instinct,' 'solid breakdown') without ever explaining to the student how to improve their reasoning process, how to structure a reflection paper, or what makes an analytical approach strong versus weak.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led through Socratic questioning. Criterion 1.2 is fully intact: the tutor builds on each student contribution and diagnoses confusion before responding. Criterion 1.3 receives a 2-point deduction under 1.3.A.b because the tutor's feedback is limited to vague affirmations without process-level direction — for example, the tutor never explains how to move from brainstorming to structured argumentation, or what distinguishes strong analytical reasoning from surface-level observation. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: responses are concise, interactive, well-anchored to the assignment, and maintain a supportive tone throughout.
